7	# Feature: DDx Consumer of the Fizeau Execution Contract
8	
9	**ID:** FEAT-006
10	**Status:** In Progress (migrating to CONTRACT-003 Fizeau execution contract)
11	**Priority:** P0
12	**Owner:** DDx Team
13	
14	## Overview
15	
16	DDx invokes LLMs through the upstream Fizeau execution contract,
17	**CONTRACT-003**, in the `~/Projects/agent` repo
18	(`docs/helix/02-design/contracts/CONTRACT-003-fizeau-service.md`).
19	
20	That contract is the entire execution boundary. DDx exposes the public
21	`ddx run` / `ddx try` / `ddx work` layers and sends requests to Fizeau for the
22	actual invocation. Upstream diagnostic/status commands may remain as
23	Fizeau-owned observability, but the retired workflow namespace is
24	not a public workflow surface and has no compatibility alias.
25	
26	## DDx-side responsibilities
27	
28	DDx owns the bead-driven workflow surface. It does NOT own:
29	- Harness orchestration (claude, codex, opencode, pi, gemini, native)
30	- Provider routing
31	- Model catalog
32	- Compaction
33	- Tool registration
34	- Session-log shape
35	
36	Those all live inside Fizeau per CONTRACT-003.
37	
38	Historical migration notes below preserve older ddx-agent naming and draft
39	shapes for traceability only. They are not the desired current contract names
40	or preferred DDx terminology.
41	
42	DDx owns:
43	
44	- **Bead-driven invocation.** `ddx try`, `ddx work`, and the server's
45	  queue-drain worker translate bead state into `ExecuteRequest` values and
46	  surface results back into the bead tracker. The queue-drain worker
47	  defaults to a long-running 30s `--poll-interval` (ddx-dc157075):
48	  workers stay alive across empty polls and surface "no ready work" as a
49	  transient `idle` substate. Operators opt out with `--once` (one-shot)
50	  or `--poll-interval=0` (legacy drain-and-exit).
51	- **Bead-attempt orchestration.** Worktree creation, base-revision pinning,
52	  result landing (merge / preserve / no-changes), gate evaluation, evidence
53	  bundle capture. The agent provides the LLM execution; DDx provides the
54	  git-aware orchestration.
55	- **Structured prompt handoff.** Review, grading, and other prompt-bearing
56	  invocations use the bounded assembly invariants from FEAT-022 and the
57	  caller-facing structured evidence envelope described in TD-033.
58	  Review-mode sessions are launched in TD-033's no-tool reviewer mode so the
59	  agent reasons over the supplied evidence instead of discovering new context
60	  at runtime.
61	- **Evidence and session capture.** DDx writes `.ddx/executions/<id>/`
62	  bundles with prompts, manifests, and result artifacts. The agent's session
63	  log path (returned in `ExecuteResponse.SessionLogPath`) is captured into
64	  the bundle.
65	- **Power policy at the request level.** DDx selects requested power bounds,
66	  effort, and permissions per bead attempt based on bead metadata, user input,
67	  and prior attempt outcomes. DDx sends `MinPower` and optionally `MaxPower`
68	  to Fizeau and leaves the concrete route to Fizeau.
69	- **Agent passthrough constraints.** DDx may accept explicit `--harness`,
70	  `--provider`, and `--model` values from the operator and pass them unchanged
71	  to Fizeau. DDx does not validate, rank, fallback, rewrite, or reason about
72	  these fields; they are opaque constraints for Fizeau to interpret.
73	- **Retry policy.** DDx owns the bead retry loop because DDx owns the evidence
74	  needed to decide whether an attempt succeeded: commits, merge/preserve
75	  result, no-changes rationale, post-run gates, review verdicts, cooldowns,
76	  and prior run metadata. On retry, DDx may raise `MinPower`. The agent owns
77	  how those power bounds map to a concrete model/provider.
78	- **Review routing request facts.** When DDx launches the default adversarial
79	  pre-close reviewer gate it uses the same `Execute` boundary, requests
80	  stronger reviewers by raising `MinPower` relative to the implementer's actual
81	  power, and supplies `role=reviewer` plus correlation metadata (`bead_id`,
82	  `attempt_id`, `session_id`, `result_rev`, `review_group_id`, reviewer slot,
83	  and implementer route facts when known). These are request facts and audit
84	  metadata, not a DDx-side routing algorithm. See ADR-024.
85	
86	DDx owns bead/worker lifecycle progress and execution evidence capture.
87	Fizeau owns transcript/progress/session rendering, provider/model discovery,
88	and session presentation. DDx is a pass-through/marshalling consumer of
89	opaque Fizeau transcript events: it may forward Fizeau `ServiceEvent`s
90	unchanged and link or copy Fizeau artifacts into the execution evidence
91	bundle, but DDx tracks those payloads only as opaque evidence. DDx keeps the
92	inner Fizeau transcript or session-log content opaque; it does not rehydrate,
93	parse, render, or semantically interpret it, and those payloads never become
94	DDx worker state. The session log payload remains opaque to DDx, which only
95	carries the envelope around it.
96	
97	## Power Intent
98	
99	DDx does not route. DDx chooses request-level power bounds and sends them to
100	Fizeau as `MinPower` and optional `MaxPower`; Fizeau resolves harness,
101	provider, endpoint, model, health, quota, fallback, and route errors.
102	
103	Power is an abstract integer scale owned by the Fizeau contract. DDx treats
104	`MinPower`/`MaxPower` as bounds on that scale, not as model identities. For
105	example, Fizeau may report:
106	
107	```text
108	running with qwen 3.6-27b (power 10)
109	```
110	
111	DDx records requested `MinPower`/`MaxPower` and the actual model/power returned
112	by Fizeau. DDx can use that evidence on a later retry to raise `MinPower`, but
113	it still does not choose the next model.
114	
115	Fizeau also exposes its available model/power catalog. DDx may read that
116	catalog to choose a `MinPower` threshold for "top model only" retries, for
117	example by requesting a lower bound at or above the lowest power among the
118	current top models. DDx must not use the catalog to pin a concrete
119	model/provider on the normal execution path.
120	
121	DDx may also pass request facts such as estimated prompt size, whether tools are
122	required, permissions, timeout values, and effort/reasoning intent. These facts
123	describe the work; they do not select a model.
124	
125	## Non-Bead Invocation Intent
126	
127	Not every layer-1 invocation comes from a bead. Artifact generation,
128	artifact regeneration, prompt comparison skills, and diagnostics can call
129	`ddx run` directly. For those calls DDx may choose work intent from the
130	artifact or operation being performed:
131	
132	- `MinPower` / optional `MaxPower` derived from the artifact's generator,
133	  media type, size, risk, or explicit operator flags
134	- permissions needed for the operation, such as read-only, workspace-write, or
135	  network access
136	- timeout and effort/reasoning intent
137	- metadata such as `produces_artifact`, artifact id, execution definition id,
138	  and governing document ids
139	- review-mode prompt envelopes that are explicitly no-tool and assembled per
140	  TD-033 / FEAT-022
141	
142	These are DDx-owned work facts, not routing decisions. DDx must not translate an
143	artifact key, media type, generator name, or execution definition into a concrete
144	harness/provider/model. The only concrete route-like fields DDx may send are
145	operator-supplied passthrough constraints, carried unchanged in the passthrough
146	envelope described below.
147	
148	## Agent Passthrough Constraints
149	
150	`--harness`, `--provider`, and `--model` are permitted on `ddx run`, `ddx try`,
151	and `ddx work` only as passthrough fields. DDx carries them in one narrow
152	request envelope and sends them to Fizeau unchanged.
153	
154	For example:
155	
156	```bash
157	ddx run --min-power 10 --model qwen36 --prompt task.md
158	```
159	
160	DDx forwards `qwen36` verbatim. Fizeau owns any fuzzy matching, alias
161	resolution, provider fallback, or typed error returned for that raw string.
162	DDx passes the raw string unchanged.
163	
164	These fields must not leak into DDx routing policy:
165	
166	- no DDx-side validation against harness/provider/model catalogs
167	- no preflight `ResolveRoute`
168	- no provider/model fallback or substitution
169	- no retry-policy branches that inspect the string values
170	- no config-driven default harness/provider/model selection
171	- no worker columns or queue selection logic that treat these as DDx concepts
172	
173	If an operator supplies passthrough constraints and DDx later retries with a
174	higher `MinPower`, DDx keeps the passthrough values unchanged. The agent
175	decides whether the power bounds and passthrough constraints are compatible and
176	reports the actual model/power or a typed error. DDx records the requested
177	passthrough values for audit, but it does not use them to select a route.
178	
179	When hard passthrough pins make the requested power bounds unsatisfiable, DDx
180	must stop with a typed terminal classification such as
181	`blocked_by_passthrough_constraint` or `agent_power_unsatisfied`. DDx records
182	the requested `MinPower`/`MaxPower`, passthrough envelope, and Fizeau-supplied
183	evidence, then reports operator action required. DDx must not remove pins,
184	choose alternatives, call `ResolveRoute` to work around the conflict, or loop on
185	higher `MinPower` values.
186	
187	The only DDx code allowed to touch raw passthrough fields is the CLI parser, the
188	single passthrough/intent value, Execute request construction, execution-record
189	persistence, and human-facing evidence rendering. Retry, queue selection, work
190	scheduling, catalog threshold helpers, and config defaulting must carry the
191	opaque value without reading `Harness`, `Provider`, or `Model`.
192	
193	`ResolveRoute` is not part of normal execution. It is allowed only for
194	operator-facing status/debug surfaces such as route-status and provider
195	diagnostics. A `RouteDecision` returned by `ResolveRoute` must never be
196	re-injected into `Execute`.
197	
198	`agent.routing.profile_ladders`, `agent.routing.model_overrides`,
199	`agent.routing.profile_priority`, and the `cheap`/`standard`/`smart` profile
200	taxonomy are not DDx execution policy. Configs or tests that rely on DDx-managed
201	ladders, profiles, or model overrides are migration debt.
202	
203	## Catalog and Status Boundary
204	
205	Model/power catalog rendering is observability. Catalog status surfaces may show
206	model names, providers, powers, and route candidates returned by the agent for
207	debugging. Retry threshold policy may consume only abstract power numbers from
208	the catalog to compute `MinPower` thresholds. Neither status nor retry code may
209	return or mutate concrete harness/provider/model pins for execution.
210	
211	## Escalation And Review Routing Boundary
212	
213	ADR-024 is the governing policy for power escalation and review routing. FEAT-006
214	owns only the request envelope: requested `MinPower` / `MaxPower`, opaque
215	passthrough constraints, role/correlation metadata, and actual route facts
216	returned by the agent. FEAT-010 owns whether a retry or review retry is
217	scheduled. FEAT-014 owns normalized usage and cost signals.
218	
219	DDx may compute the next `MinPower` floor from catalog power numbers, but it
220	must not translate that floor into a concrete model/provider or mutate
221	operator-supplied passthrough values. Review pairing uses the same boundary:
222	DDx can request stronger review and record `review-pairing-degraded` when the
223	actual reviewer route converges with the implementer, but concrete reviewer
224	selection remains inside the agent service.
225	
226	## Session Log Envelope Boundary
227	
228	The upstream agent owns the native session-log schema and any harness-specific
229	details inside it. DDx owns only the envelope around that log:
230	
231	- request id, layer, parent run id, bead id or produced artifact id
232	- requested `MinPower` / `MaxPower` and opaque passthrough constraints
233	- actual model and actual power returned by the agent
234	- pointer or copied attachment for `ExecuteResponse.SessionLogPath`
235	- DDx-owned attempt outcome, merge/preserve outcome, gates, and evidence refs
236	
237	DDx may link or copy the session log attachment for humans, but normal
238	execution policy must not render the inner session log, parse transcript
239	semantics, or infer routing, provider health, model fallbacks, or retry
240	destinations from it. DDx treats the session log as a Fizeau-owned transcript
241	artifact and only uses typed agent response fields plus DDx-owned attempt
242	evidence as policy inputs. DDx copies or links the artifact as evidence; it
243	does not present the inner session log as a DDx-rendered view.
244	
245	## Layer Ownership Migration Table
246	
247	FEAT-006 is deliberately narrow after the three-layer refactor. It describes
248	how DDx consumes the upstream agent contract and how layer-1 requests are
249	formed; it does not own worktree, queue, or CLI namespace design.
250	
251	| Area | Owner after migration |
252	| --- | --- |
253	| CONTRACT-003 Fizeau execution boundary, `ExecuteRequest` / `ExecuteResponse` consumption, `MinPower` / `MaxPower`, actual model/power recording | Stays in FEAT-006 |
254	| Opaque passthrough envelope for `--harness`, `--provider`, `--model` and the rule that DDx must not route on those fields | Stays in FEAT-006 |
255	| Non-bead layer-1 invocation intent: artifact-keyed power bounds, permissions, timeout, effort, and metadata | Stays in FEAT-006 |
256	| Session-log envelope and pointer/copy capture around the agent-owned inner log | Stays in FEAT-006 |
257	| Worktree creation, base revision pinning, bead prompt resolution, result landing, preserve refs, post-run gates, evidence bundles | Moves to FEAT-010 layer 2 (`ddx try`) |
258	| Unified `.ddx/runs/<run-id>/` substrate, layer metadata, child/parent run links, migration from `.ddx/exec-runs/` and `.ddx/executions/` | Moves to FEAT-010 |
259	| Queue iteration, no-progress detection, retry scheduling, cooldowns, stop conditions, loop records | Moves to FEAT-010 layer 3 (`ddx work`) |
260	| Public top-level command shape: `ddx run`, `ddx try`, `ddx work`, `ddx runs`, `ddx tries`, `ddx work workers` | Moves to FEAT-001 |
261	| Structural mounting of the upstream agent Cobra root under the retired DDx workflow namespace and hard-deprecation redirects for legacy workflow commands | Moves to FEAT-001, with the contract boundary referenced here |
262	
263	### Conflict-recovery structured outcomes (bead ddx-0097af14)
264	
265	Before parking a bead on `land_conflict` (or on `execution_failed` when the
266	agent produced a commit before failing), the `ddx work` drain now attempts to reuse
267	the preserved iteration ref rather than discarding the agent's work:
268	
269	1. **3-way ort auto-merge** (`git merge --no-ff -s ort -X ours`). If clean,
270	   the bead closes as `success`.
271	2. **Focused conflict-resolve agent** (if `ConflictResolver` is configured on
272	   the worker). If clean, the bead closes as `success`.
273	3. **Park with structured outcome.** Two new statuses replace the generic
274	   reopen when both recovery paths fail:
275	
276	| Status | Event kind | Meaning |
277	| --- | --- | --- |
278	| `land_conflict_unresolvable` | `land-conflict-unresolvable` | All auto-recovery failed; retryable after 15-min cooldown. |
279	| `land_conflict_needs_human` | `land-conflict-needs-human` | Focused-resolve agent returned BLOCKING; human input required. |
280	
281	Both statuses use `LandConflictCooldown` (15 min) rather than the 24h cap used
282	for `push_failed`, because land conflicts typically unblock quickly as sibling
283	beads advance the base branch.
284	
285	### Disrupted vs no-progress cooldown policy (bead ddx-5b3e57f4)
286	
287	The execute-loop cooldown branch must distinguish *worker disruption* from
288	*model gave up*. A failed attempt where the model never had a chance to commit
289	— context cancelled, executor killed by SIGTERM/SIGKILL, transport-class
290	provider error, server restart, routing preflight rejection — is NOT evidence
291	the model could not make progress on the bead. Parking such an attempt under
292	the 6h `noProgressCooldown` silently freezes important work whenever a worker
293	is disrupted by an unrelated cause.
294	
295	The loop classifies the failure via `classifyDisruption(ctx, executorErr)` and
296	sets `ExecuteBeadReport.Disrupted = true` plus a stable `DisruptionReason`
297	kind:
298	
299	| Reason | Trigger |
300	| --- | --- |
301	| `context_canceled` | `ctx.Err() == context.Canceled` (SIGINT, parent died, Stop()) |
302	| `context_deadline` | `ctx.Err() == context.DeadlineExceeded` |
303	| `transport_error` | Executor error matches a transport-class marker (connection refused/reset, gateway, EOF, TLS handshake, …) |
304	| `preflight_rejected` | Upstream `RoutePreflight` rejected the (harness, model) pair |
305	
306	When `report.Disrupted` is true:
307	
308	1. `shouldSuppressNoProgress` returns false unconditionally — the loop
309	   bypasses `SetExecutionCooldown` so the bead is immediately re-claimable.
310	2. A `disruption_detected` event is appended to the bead and emitted on the
311	   loop event sink (`{reason, detail, harness, model}`) so operators can see
312	   disruption rates and which class is firing.
313	3. Genuine no-progress (clean executor return, BaseRev == ResultRev, not
314	   Disrupted) still hits the configured `noProgressCooldown` (default 6h).
315	
316	The classifier is intentionally fuzzy on transport markers: a false positive
317	costs one quick retry; a false negative costs a 6-hour park for what was a
318	transient outage.
319	
320	## Bead-Attempt Worker Sub-task Discovery Policy
321	
322	**Design position: option (b) — surface via result.**
323	
324	Workers executing inside `ddx try` / `ddx work` bead attempts MUST NOT call
325	`ddx bead create`
326	in-band during their execution run. Creating beads in-band (option c) allows a
327	single worker to flood the queue with unreviewed children — in one observed case
328	(ddx-44236615), one worker spawned 11 P0 children without operator review. This
329	violates the architectural principle from FEAT-013: *"DDx provides primitives,
330	not orchestration. Orchestration policy stays in HELIX and other workflow tools."*
331	
332	Workers MAY surface discovered sub-tasks as structured data in `result.json`
333	under a `discovered_subtasks` array. Each entry is a lightweight object with at
334	minimum a `title` and optional `description`, `labels`, and `priority` fields.
335	The `ddx work` drain or supervisor reads this array from the result bundle and passes
336	it to the workflow tool (HELIX or operator) for decomposition decisions. The
337	supervisor decides whether, when, and at what priority to file new beads — DDx
338	does not do this automatically.
339	
340	### Rationale
341	
342	| Option | Description | Problem |
343	| --- | --- | --- |
344	| (a) Append to parent | Worker writes sub-tasks as notes or events on the parent bead | Loses structure; sub-tasks are not queryable or executable without manual decomposition |
345	| (b) Surface via result (**chosen**) | Agent emits `discovered_subtasks` in result.json; supervisor decides | Gates tracker mutations at an explicit decision point; aligns with "primitives not orchestration" |
346	| (c) Create in-band (former behavior) | Worker calls `ddx bead create` directly | Unreviewed children flood the queue; P0 tagging without operator review; violates workflow ownership boundary |
347	
348	Option (b) preserves the operator control point required by workflow tools while
349	providing structure that automation can act on. It is the only option consistent
350	with the FEAT-013 boundary.
351	
352	### Result schema extension
353	
354	`result.json` gains an optional `discovered_subtasks` array. The bead-attempt
355	orchestrator writes it from the agent's structured output when present. The
356	supervisor MUST NOT act on this field automatically; it is surfaced as an
357	observation for the workflow tool.
358	
359	```json
360	{
361	  "status": "success",
362	  "detail": "...",
363	  "discovered_subtasks": [
364	    {
365	      "title": "Add read-coverage for X",
366	      "description": "Optional detail about what is needed",
367	      "labels": ["area:tests"],
368	      "priority": "P1"
369	    }
370	  ]
371	}
372	```
373	
374	If the agent does not emit `discovered_subtasks`, the field is absent. The
375	`ddx work` drain treats an absent or empty array identically — no automatic bead
376	creation occurs in either case.
377	
378	### Enforcement
379	
380	The bead-attempt prompt delivered to the agent (via the prompt rationalizer)
381	MUST include an explicit instruction that workers must not call `ddx bead create`
382	and must instead emit `discovered_subtasks` in their result if sub-tasks are
383	identified. This constraint is part of the prompt contract; it is not enforced
384	by the CLI at runtime.
385	
386	## Malformed-Bead Remediation Decision (beads ddx-7eab13a6 era)
387	
388	**Decision: close and refile.**
389	
390	The layer-1 worktree-path bug fixed in ddx-7eab13a6 caused 22 beads to be
391	created with IDs of the form `.execute-bead-wt-<parent>-<timestamp>-<random>-<hex>`
392	(the worktree directory path was mistakenly used as the bead ID). These beads
393	are invalid: their IDs do not match the `ddx-<8hex>` format expected by all DDx
394	CLI commands, and the IDs appear in git commit trailers as malformed values.
395	
396	Repair in-place is not safe. Bead IDs are immutable identifiers embedded in git
397	commit trailers (`Ddx-Attempt-Id` etc.), inter-bead dependency edges, and the
398	JSONL append-only store. Renaming an existing bead ID requires rewriting
399	history, which violates the merge policy recorded in project memory.
400	
401	The operator (human or supervising workflow tool) should:
402	1. Inspect each malformed bead's description to determine whether the underlying
403	   work is still valid.
404	2. If valid: refile a new bead with the same title and description, using
405	   `ddx bead create`. The new bead will receive a correct ID.
406	3. Close the malformed bead with `ddx bead close <id> --reason "malformed-id
407	   refiled as <new-id>"`.
408	4. If the work is no longer needed: close the malformed bead with reason
409	   `malformed-id obsolete`.
410	
411	No automated bulk remediation is performed. The 22 beads are left open pending
412	operator triage. A future `ddx bead validate` command (not yet implemented) can
413	flag structurally invalid bead IDs to aid discovery.
414	
415	## Asking Fizeau for changes
416	
417	When DDx needs new behavior from the service — a new method, a new field on
418	`ExecuteRequest`, a new event type, a new policy knob — file a PR against
419	CONTRACT-003 in the Fizeau repo. Maintainers decide whether the surface grows.
420	
421	Do not import agent internal packages. They live under `internal/` and the
422	Go compiler blocks external imports after agent v0.5.0 ships.
423	
424	### Operator-prompt bead type (Story 15)
425	
426	Free-form prompts submitted from the web UI are persisted as beads with
427	`issueType: operator-prompt` and drained by the standard execute-loop. The
428	title is the prompt's first line, the body is the full prompt verbatim,
429	default labels are `kind:operator-prompt,source:web-ui`, and the AC section
430	is auto-generated from a template. These beads run with the same
431	harness/profile/tier as regular beads but skip the structural AC check
432	(which assumes pre-authored AC). They cannot mutate other operator-prompt
433	beads — a hard rule enforced at the bead store, not at planning time.
434	
435	The trust contract, audit-as-bead model, multi-node delegation policy,
436	prompt-injection threat model, and allowed-mutation scope are captured in
437	**ADR-021: Operator-Prompt Beads as the Web Write Path**.
438	
439	## Worker Contract (per ADR-022)
440	
441	Workers are **autonomous against the bead store**. They have one mode of
442	operation: read the bead store, pick the next eligible bead via the
443	worker-side picker, claim it atomically, execute via `try.Attempt`, write
444	evidence locally, and close the bead through the store. **All of this
445	happens regardless of whether `ddx-server` is running.** The bead store is
446	the source of truth; the server is an optional, value-added coordinator
447	that provides centralized observability — never a dependency for correct
448	worker operation.
449	
450	ADR-022 (rev 5, commit `484b9a08`) is the governing decision. Earlier
451	revisions (1–3) explored a server-orchestrates direction in which
452	workers were long-lived API clients of the server; rev 4 reversed that,
453	and rev 5 finalized the autonomous-default + best-effort-mirror design
454	described here.
455	
456	### Server probe goroutine
457	
458	In parallel with the autonomous work loop, every worker runs a small
459	"server probe" goroutine that detects whether a server is reachable via
460	`~/.local/share/ddx/server.addr` and tracks a Connected ↔ NotConnected
461	state machine. Probe cadence: immediate first probe on startup, then a
462	jittered 30s steady-state interval (10s minimum, 5min maximum); five
463	consecutive failures back the rate off to 5min, resetting to 30s on the
464	next success. A 410 unknown_worker reply triggers re-registration within
465	the same cycle.
466	
467	### Best-effort mirror
468	
469	When the worker is in the Connected state it mirrors bead events and
470	results to the server's ingestion endpoints best-effort. Mirror failures
471	(timeout, connection refused, 5xx) are logged locally and the worker
472	continues; the bead's local event log is the authoritative copy. The
473	worker-server boundary collapses to a small set of endpoints, all backed
474	by `requireTrusted` (per ADR-006):
475	
476	- `POST /api/workers/register` — emitted on every NotConnected → Connected
477	  transition (not only at startup). Body is a thin identity envelope
478	  (`project_root`, `harness`, `model`, `executor_pid`, `executor_host`,
479	  `started_at`); response carries a correlation `worker_id`. There is no
480	  session token, no claim lease, no heartbeat — none of those concepts
481	  exist because the worker does not depend on the server.
482	- `POST /api/workers/<id>/event` — best-effort mirror of any bead event
483	  the worker would already write to the local event log; uses the
484	  existing bead event `kind`/`body` shape. 204 on success.
485	- `POST /api/workers/<id>/backfill` — replays an in-memory ring buffer
486	  (cap 200 events) of events emitted while the worker was NotConnected;
487	  emitted on each NotConnected → Connected transition. Overflow drops
488	  oldest silently and the worker is marked `had_dropped_backfill: true`
489	  for UI display; bead-local logs remain complete.
490	- `POST /api/workers/<id>/disconnect` — graceful shutdown signal;
491	  best-effort, not required for correctness.
492	
493	### Freshness states
494	
495	The server's view of workers is **derived** from these reports —
496	eventually consistent, never authoritative. The server-side workers panel
497	surfaces three freshness states based on `last_event_at` relative to the
498	probe interval:
499	
500	| State | Definition |
501	| --- | --- |
502	| `connected` | Event in the last 2× probe interval |
503	| `stale` | Event between 2× and 10× probe interval |
504	| `disconnected` | No event in > 10× probe interval (worker presumed dead) |
505	
506	UI also surfaces `mirror_failures_count` and `had_dropped_backfill` so
507	operators can spot healthy-but-lossy workers, and labels reported worker
508	identity as "trusted-peer reported, not authoritative" to reflect the
509	ADR-006 trust model.
510	
511	### Cancel via bead extra
512	
513	Operator-initiated cancel is **not** a direct API call to the worker.
514	The server's `/api/beads/<id>/cancel` endpoint (or a direct bead-store
515	write) sets `extra.cancel-requested: true` on the bead. Workers honor
516	the marker at the next safe point:
517	
518	- The worker re-reads the bead's `extra` map every 10s during long
519	  attempts (mid-attempt poll). On `cancel-requested: true` it aborts at
520	  the next LLM turn / git operation boundary and reports
521	  `preserved_for_review` with reason `operator_cancel`.
522	- A worker starting work on a bead that is already cancel-marked
523	  immediately reports `preserved_for_review` and skips the attempt.
524	- Idempotency: the worker writes `cancel-honored: true` so a re-applied
525	  marker does not re-trigger.
526	- Worst-case latency: ~10s plus the current LLM turn duration; operators
527	  needing faster cancel use OS signals.
528	
529	### Restart and crash behavior
530	
531	- **Server restart or crash.** The worker's next mirror POST fails;
532	  worker logs the transition and continues working. On the next
533	  successful probe it re-registers and resumes mirroring; the in-memory
534	  ring buffer feeds backfill. No in-flight work is at risk because the
535	  worker never depended on the server.
536	- **Worker exit.** No reclaim machinery is needed: the bead store's
537	  atomic claim is the only claim primitive. Other workers contend on
538	  the same store-level CAS that has worked correctly for as long as DDx
539	  has had a bead store.
540	
541	### `--local` deprecation
542	
543	Today's `--local` semantics — autonomous behavior with no server — are
544	the always-on default. The flag is preserved as a deprecation no-op for
545	one alpha release (`v0.6.2-alphaN+1` after the rev 5 work lands) and
546	then deleted. Operators wanting to suppress the probe simply do not run
547	a server.
548	
549	### Compatibility writers
550	
551	`.ddx/workers/` (spec.json + status.json) is preserved as a fallback
552	source of truth for Fizeau observability for one alpha release lag, then
553	deprecated once the doctor migrates to the server's derived view. The
554	server's append-only event log lives at `.ddx/server/worker-events.jsonl`.
555	
556	The attempt-orchestration responsibilities listed under "DDx-side
557	responsibilities" above (worktree creation, base-revision pinning,
558	result landing, evidence capture) all execute inside the worker process.
559	The server, when present, observes; it does not run agents itself, hold
560	authoritative claim state, gate worker startup, or assign beads to
561	workers. See ADR-022 for the full contract, picker rationale,
562	freshness/state tables, threat model, compatibility analysis, and
563	implementation roadmap.
564	
565	## Migration status
566	
567	DDx is currently migrating from the legacy DDx-internal routing/harness
568	implementation to the CONTRACT-003 surface. Migration epic: `ddx-ac5c7fdb`.
569	Pre-migration, FEAT-006 documented harness orchestration responsibilities
570	that have since moved upstream.
571	
572	## Design References
573	
574	- `docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-006-agent-session-capture.md` — session log shape and evidence bundles
575	- `docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-013-multi-agent-coordination.md` — worktree-aware dispatch, concurrent bead safety
576	- `docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-014-token-awareness.md` — provider availability, quota, and signal model
577	- `docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-016-process-metrics.md` — bead-level cost and rework derivation
578	- `docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-020-multi-machine-coordinator-topology.md` — host+user coordinator scoping
579	- `docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-021-service-backed-multi-node-topology.md` — service-backed node topology
580	- `docs/helix/02-design/technical-designs/TD-006-agent-session-capture.md` — session log and artifact format
581	- `docs/helix/02-design/technical-designs/TD-010-executions.md` — execution attempt bundles at `.ddx/executions/`
582	- Historical thin-consumer migration plan — superseded by the current Fizeau consumer spec
583	- `docs/helix/03-test/test-plans/TP-006-agent-session-capture.md` — session capture test coverage
584	- `docs/helix/03-test/test-plans/TP-014-token-awareness.md` — token-awareness coverage
585	- `docs/helix/03-test/test-plans/TP-020-fizeau-boundary-and-pass-through.md` — DDx boundary coverage for raw passthrough and no-local-routing behavior
586	- `docs/helix/02-design/adr/ADR-021-operator-prompt-beads-web-write-path.md` — operator-prompt beads as the web write path (Story 15)
587	- `docs/helix/02-design/adr/ADR-022-worker-client-server-architecture.md` — workers as long-lived API clients; server-restart preserves in-flight work
588	- `docs/helix/02-design/adr/ADR-024-power-escalation-and-review-routing.md` — DDx power escalation, review routing, and cost-cap policy boundary
589

1	---
2	name: adversarial-review
3	description: Run an adversarial review of a plan, spec, bead, or PR by dispatching to multiple harnesses with adversarial framing and aggregating findings. Use when you want pressure-testing against a single-model blindspot, high-stakes review, or multi-round critique before committing to a direction.
4	---
5	
6	# Adversarial Review
7	
8	A workflow skill for pressure-testing a target (plan, spec, bead, code change,
9	or PR) by dispatching adversarial review prompts to multiple harnesses and
10	aggregating the findings. Replaces ad-hoc inline quorum invocations with a
11	structured, repeatable workflow.
12	
13	## When to use
14	
15	- **Plans and specs** before breaking into beads — catch ambiguity and missing
16	  constraints early.
17	- **High-stakes beads** — migrations, auth, API contracts, security surface.
18	- **Controversial changes** — one model's opinion is not enough; different model
19	  families catch different failure modes.
20	- **Multi-round refinement** — run 2–3 rounds; use findings to revise; re-run
21	  until BLOCKING count drops to zero.
22	
23	Single-harness bead review (`ddx bead review <id>`) is fine for routine work.
24	Reserve adversarial-review for the cases that justify the cost.
25	
26	## Workflow
27	
28	### 1. Prepare the target
29	
30	Gather the content to review into a single prompt file. Include:
31	
32	- The artifact being reviewed (plan text, spec section, bead description + AC,
33	  or diff / PR summary).
34	- The question you want pressure-tested ("Is this design complete?", "What are
35	  the failure modes?", "What does this spec leave undefined?").
36	- Any governing artifacts the reviewer should check against (FEAT-\* IDs,
37	  CONTRACT-\* IDs, ADR links).
38	
39	Write the prompt to a file, e.g. `review-target.md`:
40	
41	```markdown
42	## Target
43	
44	<paste plan, spec excerpt, or diff>
45	
46	## Governing artifacts
47	
48	- FEAT-010: docs/helix/01-frame/features/FEAT-010-task-execution.md
49	- (add others)
50	
51	## Review question
52	
53	Find BLOCKING issues: ambiguities, missing constraints, design gaps,
54	internal contradictions, or anything that would cause implementation
55	rework. Be adversarial — your job is to find problems, not validate.
56	```
57	
58	### 2. Dispatch to multiple harnesses
59	
60	Run each harness in parallel using `ddx run --harness <name>`.
61	Use `--min-power 10` (or `--harness` for explicit control). Route to
62	harnesses from different model families for maximum coverage.
63	
64	```bash
65	# Two-harness adversarial dispatch (parallel)
66	ddx run --harness codex --min-power 10 \
67	  --prompt review-target.md \
68	  > findings-codex.md &
69	
70	ddx run --harness claude --min-power 10 \
71	  --prompt review-target.md \
72	  > findings-claude.md &
73	
74	wait
75	```
76	
77	For three harnesses:
78	
79	```bash
80	ddx run --harness codex  --min-power 10 --prompt review-target.md > findings-codex.md  &
81	ddx run --harness claude --min-power 10 --prompt review-target.md > findings-claude.md &
82	ddx run --harness gemini --min-power 10 --prompt review-target.md > findings-gemini.md &
83	wait
84	```
85	
86	### 3. Require structured output
87	
88	Each harness must produce findings in this format. Include this contract
89	in the prompt:
90	
91	```markdown
92	## Output contract
93	
94	Produce findings as:
95	
96	### Findings
97	
98	| Severity | Area | Finding |
99	|---|---|---|
100	| BLOCKING | <area> | <specific issue> |
101	| WARNING  | <area> | <specific issue> |
102	| NOTE     | <area> | <observation> |
103	
104	### Verdict: APPROVE | REQUEST_CHANGES | BLOCK
105	
106	### Summary
107	<2–4 sentences>
108	```
109	
110	A finding with no evidence is invalid. Cite the specific line, section,
111	or gap that caused the finding. "Looks fine" is not a finding.
112	
113	### 4. Aggregate
114	
115	Read all findings files and collate:
116	
117	1. **BLOCKING** items from any harness → escalate immediately. One BLOCKING
118	   is enough to stop.
119	2. **WARNING** items with ≥2 harnesses in agreement → treat as BLOCKING.
120	3. **WARNING** items unique to one harness → review manually, weigh cost.
121	4. **NOTE** items → log; address in a follow-up pass or ignore.
122	
123	Surface disagreements explicitly. If harness A says BLOCKING and harness B
124	says NOTE for the same area, show both verdicts — do not silently collapse
125	to the weaker one.
126	
127	```bash
128	# Combine findings for review
129	cat findings-codex.md findings-claude.md > findings-combined.md
130	```
131	
132	### 5. Iterate until clean
133	
134	Revise the target based on BLOCKING findings. Re-run the adversarial review
135	against the revised version. Typical well-scoped plans converge in 2–3
136	rounds. Stop when:
137	
138	- No harness returns a BLOCKING verdict, AND
139	- All WARNINGs have been explicitly accepted or addressed.
140	
141	Record the round count and final verdict in the bead evidence:
142	
143	```bash
144	ddx bead evidence add <id> --type review --body findings-combined.md
145	```
146	
147	## Adversarial framing
148	
149	The adversarial framing instruction to include in every review prompt:
150	
151	> You are a critic, not a validator. Your job is to find every way this
152	> could fail, every constraint it leaves undefined, every assumption it
153	> bakes in without stating, every interface it leaves ambiguous. A BLOCKING
154	> finding is anything that would cause implementation rework, a migration
155	> hazard, or a spec gap that agents will interpret differently. Do not
156	> balance criticism with praise — a useful adversarial review is entirely
157	> about what is wrong.
158	
159	## Personas
160	
161	Bind a `specification-enforcer` persona for the strictest checks:
162	
163	```bash
164	ddx run --harness codex \
165	  --persona specification-enforcer \
166	  --prompt review-target.md
167	```
168	
169	The `specification-enforcer` persona refuses drift from governing artifacts
170	and surfaces spec gaps others miss.
171	
172	## Anti-patterns
173	
174	- **Single-harness adversarial review.** The point is cross-model coverage.
175	  One harness is bead review, not adversarial review.
176	- **No structured output contract.** Free-form critique is unaggregateable.
177	  Always include the findings table + verdict contract in the prompt.
178	- **Collapsing disagreements.** If harnesses disagree, show both verdicts.
179	  Hiding disagreement defeats the purpose.
180	- **Running adversarial review on routine beads.** Reserve for high-stakes
181	  or controversial work. Routine beads use `ddx bead review`.
182	- **Stopping after one round.** One round finds problems. Iteration verifies
183	  that the revision actually resolves them.
184	
185	## CLI reference
186	
187	```bash
188	# Parallel adversarial dispatch
189	ddx run --harness codex  --min-power 10 --prompt review-target.md > findings-codex.md  &
190	ddx run --harness claude --min-power 10 --prompt review-target.md > findings-claude.md &
191	wait
192	
193	# With persona
194	ddx run --harness codex --persona specification-enforcer \
195	  --prompt review-target.md > findings-codex.md
196	
197	# Store findings as evidence
198	ddx bead evidence add <id> --type review --body findings-combined.md
199	
200	# Bead review (routine, single-harness — not this skill)
201	ddx bead review <id>
202	ddx bead review <id> --execute --harness claude
203	```
204

1	---
2	name: compare-prompts
3	description: Dispatch the same prompt across N agent invocations (different harnesses, models, personas, or prompt variants) and aggregate the results into a single comparison report. Use when you need to benchmark prompt variants, run a quorum-style consensus check, compare model outputs side-by-side, or replay a prompt under altered conditions. Replaces the removed core quorum flag and benchmark CLI.
4	---
5	
6	# Compare Prompts
7	
8	A workflow skill for N-arm dispatch and result aggregation. Composes
9	`ddx run` (the layer-1 invocation primitive) into a comparison
10	matrix: same prompt, varied conditions, structured aggregation.
11	
12	This skill replaces two retired surfaces:
13	
14	- the removed inline quorum flag.
15	- the retired benchmark CLI from the old task-dispatch namespace.
16	
17	Both collapse into the same workflow shape: dispatch N invocations,
18	aggregate their outputs, render a verdict.
19	
20	## When to use
21	
22	- **Prompt benchmarking** — compare two prompt variants on the same
23	  task to decide which one ships.
24	- **Model comparison** — same prompt across `claude` / `codex` /
25	  `gemini` (or different models within one harness) to see where
26	  outputs diverge.
27	- **Consensus checks (quorum)** — run a review prompt across N
28	  harnesses and require majority/unanimous agreement before acting.
29	- **Persona comparison** — same prompt under different personas to
30	  see how role framing changes the answer.
31	- **Replay under altered conditions** — re-run a prior prompt with a
32	  changed model, power bound, or context to A/B a hypothesis.
33	
34	For pressure-testing a plan or spec with adversarial framing, prefer
35	the `adversarial-review` skill — it layers a critic-only prompt
36	contract on top of this same N-arm shape.
37	
38	## Workflow
39	
40	### 1. Define the comparison axis
41	
42	Pick exactly one axis to vary. Mixing axes makes results
43	uninterpretable.
44	
45	| Axis | Vary | Hold constant |
46	|---|---|---|
47	| harness | `--harness claude`, `--harness codex`, `--harness gemini` | prompt, power bounds, persona |
48	| model | `--model <a>`, `--model <b>` | harness, prompt, persona |
49	| prompt | prompt-v1.md, prompt-v2.md | harness, model, persona |
50	| persona | `--persona reviewer`, `--persona architect` | harness, prompt, model |
51	| power | `--min-power 1`, `--min-power 10` | harness, prompt, persona |
52	
53	If you must vary two axes (e.g., harness × model), enumerate the full
54	matrix explicitly and label each arm.
55	
56	### 2. Author the prompt
57	
58	Write a single prompt file. Include an **output contract** so each
59	arm produces structurally comparable output:
60	
61	```markdown
62	## Task
63	
64	<the actual ask — code review, plan critique, design question, etc.>
65	
66	## Output contract
67	
68	Produce your answer as:
69	
70	### Verdict: <one line>
71	
72	### Reasoning
73	<2–6 sentences>
74	
75	### Specific findings
76	- <finding 1>
77	- <finding 2>
78	```
79	
80	Without an output contract, aggregation degenerates into freeform
81	prose comparison. The contract is what makes N-arm dispatch
82	mechanically aggregateable.
83	
84	### 3. Dispatch the arms in parallel
85	
86	Run each arm as a backgrounded `ddx run`, capturing stdout
87	to a per-arm file. Wait for all arms before aggregating.
88	
89	```bash
90	PROMPT=prompt.md
91	OUT=compare-$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S)
92	mkdir -p "$OUT"
93	
94	# Three-arm harness comparison
95	ddx run --harness claude --min-power 10 --prompt "$PROMPT" > "$OUT/claude.md" 2> "$OUT/claude.err" &
96	ddx run --harness codex  --min-power 10 --prompt "$PROMPT" > "$OUT/codex.md"  2> "$OUT/codex.err"  &
97	ddx run --harness gemini --min-power 10 --prompt "$PROMPT" > "$OUT/gemini.md" 2> "$OUT/gemini.err" &
98	wait
99	```
100	
101	Per-arm conventions:
102	
103	- One file per arm, named for the varied axis value (`claude.md`,
104	  `codex.md`, …) — not `arm1.md` / `arm2.md`.
105	- Capture stderr separately. Failures in one arm should not silently
106	  drop a result row from the aggregate.
107	- Tag arms with the full invocation in a header comment when the
108	  filename alone doesn't disambiguate (e.g., model+persona variants).
109	
110	### 4. Verify each arm produced output
111	
112	Before aggregating, confirm each arm exited cleanly and produced
113	output that satisfies the prompt's output contract. An arm that
114	errored or returned empty must be marked as such — never silently
115	omitted.
116	
117	```bash
118	for arm in "$OUT"/*.md; do
119	  if [ ! -s "$arm" ]; then
120	    echo "ARM FAILED: $arm"
121	  fi
122	done
123	```
124	
125	### 5. Aggregate
126	
127	Aggregate per the comparison axis. The aggregation policy depends on
128	the use case:
129	
130	**Quorum (consensus check):**
131	
132	- `unanimous` — every arm must produce the same verdict.
133	- `majority` — strictly more than half the arms agree.
134	- `any` — at least one arm produced the verdict (lowest bar).
135	
136	Surface disagreement explicitly. If 2 of 3 arms say APPROVE and 1
137	says BLOCK, the report shows all three verdicts plus the policy
138	outcome. Hidden disagreement defeats the purpose of multi-arm
139	dispatch.
140	
141	**Benchmark (prompt or model variant comparison):**
142	
143	- Tabulate the structured output across arms (verdict + findings).
144	- Flag arms that diverged from the others on the verdict line.
145	- Note quality differentials — but do not pick a winner mechanically;
146	  human judgment closes the comparison.
147	
148	**Replay (altered-conditions re-run):**
149	
150	- Compare the new arm against the prior baseline arm directly.
151	- Surface the diff in verdict and findings; do not collapse to a
152	  single "improved / regressed" label.
153	
154	### 6. Render the comparison report
155	
156	Combine arms into a single Markdown report with a row per arm and
157	the structured output side-by-side:
158	
159	```bash
160	cat > "$OUT/report.md" <<EOF
161	# Compare-prompts report
162	
163	- Axis: harness
164	- Prompt: $PROMPT
165	- MinPower: 10
166	- Arms: claude, codex, gemini
167	
168	## Per-arm output
169	
170	### claude
171	$(cat "$OUT/claude.md")
172	
173	### codex
174	$(cat "$OUT/codex.md")
175	
176	### gemini
177	$(cat "$OUT/gemini.md")
178	
179	## Aggregation
180	
181	<verdict table + policy outcome>
182	EOF
183	```
184	
185	If the comparison is associated with a bead (e.g., a quorum review
186	of the bead's diff), attach the report as evidence:
187	
188	```bash
189	ddx bead evidence add <id> --type review --body "$OUT/report.md"
190	```
191	
192	## Replacing Removed Quorum Flags
193	
194	The retired inline quorum flags map to this workflow:
195	
196	| Old | New |
197	|---|---|
198	| old harness list | three parallel `ddx run --harness <name>` calls |
199	| old majority policy | aggregation step 5, "majority" policy |
200	| old unanimous policy | aggregation step 5, "unanimous" policy |
201	| inline aggregated stdout | `report.md` rendered by step 6 |
202	
203	The skill formalizes what core used to do inline. Behavioral parity
204	requires choosing the same harness set, the same policy, and routing each arm
205	through the same `MinPower`/`MaxPower` bounds.
206	
207	## Replacing `agent benchmark`
208	
209	The retired benchmark CLI mapped a prompt across a fixed matrix and rendered a
210	results table. The skill subsumes it by:
211	
212	- Authoring the matrix as the per-arm dispatch list in step 3.
213	- Reusing the output contract in step 2 to keep arms aggregateable.
214	- Rendering the results table in step 6.
215	
216	For benchmarking across many prompts (a prompt suite), see the
217	`benchmark-suite` skill — it composes this skill across a prompt
218	matrix.
219	
220	## Anti-patterns
221	
222	- **Mixing axes silently.** Varying both harness and prompt at once
223	  hides which variable produced the difference. Pick one axis, or
224	  enumerate the full matrix.
225	- **No output contract.** Free-form arm output is not
226	  aggregateable. Always include the structured output contract in
227	  the prompt.
228	- **Dropping failed arms.** A failed arm is data. Mark it as failed
229	  in the report — do not silently aggregate over the survivors.
230	- **Picking a winner mechanically.** Quorum aggregation is
231	  mechanical (policy → verdict). Benchmark aggregation requires
232	  human judgment on quality, not a counting rule.
233	- **One-arm "comparison".** A single dispatch is `ddx run`,
234	  not a comparison. This skill requires N ≥ 2.
235	- **Reusing `arm1.md` / `arm2.md` filenames.** Name files for the
236	  varied axis value so the report is self-describing.
237	
238	## CLI reference
239	
240	```bash
241	# Three-harness quorum (majority policy)
242	ddx run --harness claude --min-power 10 --prompt prompt.md > out/claude.md &
243	ddx run --harness codex  --min-power 10 --prompt prompt.md > out/codex.md  &
244	ddx run --harness gemini --min-power 10 --prompt prompt.md > out/gemini.md &
245	wait
246	
247	# Two-prompt benchmark (one harness, varying prompt)
248	ddx run --harness claude --min-power 10 --prompt prompt-v1.md > out/v1.md &
249	ddx run --harness claude --min-power 10 --prompt prompt-v2.md > out/v2.md &
250	wait
251	
252	# Two-persona comparison
253	ddx run --harness claude --persona reviewer  --prompt prompt.md > out/reviewer.md  &
254	ddx run --harness claude --persona architect --prompt prompt.md > out/architect.md &
255	wait
256	
257	# Attach the comparison report as bead evidence
258	ddx bead evidence add <id> --type review --body out/report.md
259	```
260	
261	## Related skills
262	
263	- `adversarial-review` — N-arm dispatch with adversarial critic
264	  framing layered on top.
265	- `benchmark-suite` — composes `compare-prompts` across a prompt
266	  matrix for suite-level evaluation.
267	- `replay-bead` — re-run a prior bead's prompt under altered
268	  conditions; uses the replay aggregation shape from step 5.
269

1	# Task Dispatch — Power, Persona, Passthrough
2	
3	> **Debugging routing?** Routing lives entirely in Fizeau. See `CONTRACT-003-fizeau-service` (in the agent repo, `docs/helix/02-design/contracts/`) for the public surface; routing internals are inside that module.
4	
5	DDx invokes task execution through the upstream Fizeau service. DDx does not
6	choose harnesses, providers, endpoints, or models on the normal work path. It
7	describes the work, requests an abstract power level, and lets Fizeau route.
8	
9	## Power bounds
10	
11	Normal execution should use power bounds:
12	
13	```bash
14	ddx run --min-power 10 --prompt task.md
15	```
16	
17	Power is an integer scale exposed by the Fizeau contract. DDx passes
18	`MinPower` and optional `MaxPower` bounds and treats them as ordering
19	constraints, not as model identities. Fizeau reports what it actually used:
20	
21	```text
22	running with qwen 3.6-27b (power 10)
23	```
24	
25	DDx owns bead retry policy. It decides whether an attempt succeeded using DDx
26	evidence: commits, no-changes rationale, merge/preserve result, gates, review
27	verdicts, and cooldowns. If a retry is allowed, DDx may raise `MinPower`.
28	
29	Fizeau owns route selection within those requested bounds. DDx may query the
30	available model/power catalog to choose a `MinPower` threshold such as "top
31	models only", but it must not pin a concrete model/provider on the normal work
32	path.
33	
34	## Passthrough constraints
35	
36	`--harness`, `--provider`, and `--model` are allowed only as passthrough
37	constraints:
38	
39	```bash
40	ddx run --min-power 10 --model qwen3.6-27b --prompt task.md
41	```
42	
43	DDx sends these values to Fizeau unchanged. DDx does not validate them,
44	score them, use them for queue policy, rewrite them on retry, or use them as a
45	fallback mechanism. If the values conflict with requested power bounds or
46	availability, Fizeau owns the typed error or actual route. DDx stops on
47	hard-pin exhaustion; it does not remove pins, widen pins, call `ResolveRoute`,
48	or retry in a loop.
49	
50	## Execution layering
51	
52	The intended command layering is:
53	
54	- `ddx run` — one Fizeau `Execute` call with requested `MinPower`/`MaxPower`.
55	  No `ResolveRoute`.
56	- `ddx try <bead>` — one bead attempt layered over `run`.
57	- `ddx work` — queue drain and retry policy layered over `try`.
58	
59	`ResolveRoute` is status/debug-only. Do not use it in `run`, `try`, or `work`,
60	and never feed a `RouteDecision` back into `Execute`.
61	
62	## Personas
63	
64	Personas are reusable AI personality templates. DDx injects a
65	persona's body as a system-prompt addendum to `ddx run` —
66	same persona, same behavior across every harness, because the
67	harness sees a flat system prompt, not a persona file.
68	
69	### Default roster
70	
71	The default `ddx` plugin ships five role-focused personas:
72	
73	- `code-reviewer` — security-first review with structured verdicts
74	- `test-engineer` — stubs-over-mocks, real-e2e, baselined
75	  performance, testing pyramid as shape not ratios
76	- `implementer` — YAGNI / KISS / DOWITYTD, ships tests with code
77	- `architect` — opinionated on when to reach for each pattern
78	  (monolith-first, data-model-first)
79	- `specification-enforcer` — refuses drift from governing artifacts
80	
81	See `.ddx/plugins/ddx/personas/README.md` for the quality bar and
82	authoring guidance. Projects can install additional personas via
83	plugins.
84	
85	### Using a persona
86	
87	Directly at invocation time:
88	
89	```bash
90	ddx run --persona code-reviewer --prompt review.md
91	```
92	
93	Or by binding a role in `.ddx/config.yaml`:
94	
95	```yaml
96	persona_bindings:
97	  code-reviewer: code-reviewer
98	  test-engineer: test-engineer
99	  architect: architect
100	```
101	
102	Workflows then reference the role abstractly (e.g., "dispatch to
103	the `code-reviewer` role"), and DDx resolves the binding at
104	dispatch time.
105	
106	```bash
107	ddx persona list                # available personas
108	ddx persona show <name>         # persona body + frontmatter
109	ddx persona bind <role> <name>  # set a binding
110	ddx persona bindings            # current role → persona map
111	```
112	
113	## Quorum
114	
115	For multi-agent review or adversarial checks, use a workflow skill composition
116	such as `compare-prompts`, covered in `reference/review.md`:
117	
118	```bash
119	ddx run --persona code-reviewer --prompt review.md
120	ddx run --persona architect --prompt review.md
121	```
122	
123	## Getting more capabilities
124	
125	`ddx install <name>` adds plugins to the project. Plugins can ship
126	personas, prompts, patterns, templates, and workflow skills.
127	
128	```bash
129	ddx install helix              # HELIX workflow plugin
130	ddx search <term>              # discover available plugins
131	ddx installed                  # list installed plugins
132	ddx uninstall <name>           # remove
133	```
134	
135	Custom personas go in `.ddx/plugins/<plugin>/personas/<name>.md`
136	(or directly in `library/personas/<name>.md` for local-only use).
137	See the personas README for the authoring quality bar.
138	
139	## Anti-patterns
140	
141	- **Stacking power bounds and `--model` / `--harness` casually**. Normal DDx
142	  work should request `MinPower`. Use passthrough pins only for explicit
143	  operator constraints; DDx must not interpret them.
144	- **Hardcoding a harness name in a workflow**. Workflow should
145	  dispatch by power or role binding; letting each project route through its
146	  agent configuration is the point of DDx's agent boundary.
147	- **Persona for everything**. Personas add a system-prompt addendum;
148	  they don't make a bad prompt good. Start with a clear prompt;
149	  reach for a persona when you want consistent style/standards
150	  across invocations.
151	- **Persona files in skill directories**. Personas live in
152	  `library/personas/` or `.ddx/plugins/<plugin>/personas/`, not in
153	  `.claude/skills/` or `.agents/skills/`. Don't mix the two.
154	
155	## CLI reference
156	
157	```bash
158	# Dispatch
159	ddx run --min-power 10 --prompt task.md
160	ddx run --top-power --prompt task.md
161	ddx run --min-power 10 --max-power 20 --prompt task.md
162	ddx run --min-power 10 --provider openrouter --model qwen3.6-27b --prompt task.md
163	ddx run --persona code-reviewer --prompt review.md
164	
165	# Introspection
166	ddx doctor                              # environment health
167	ddx status                              # project drift / stale docs
168	ddx bead status                         # queue state
169	# Fizeau diagnostics, if exposed by the project, remain separate from DDx verbs.
170	
171	# Personas
172	ddx persona list
173	ddx persona show <name>
174	ddx persona bind <role> <persona>
175	ddx persona bindings
176	
177	# Plugin install
178	ddx install <plugin>
179	ddx search <term>
180	ddx installed
181	ddx uninstall <name>
182	```

1	---
2	name: ddx
3	description: Operates the DDx toolkit for document-driven development. Covers beads (work items), the queue, executions, agents, harnesses, personas, reviews, spec-id. Use when the user says "do work", "drain the queue", "run the next bead", "execute a bead", "review this", "review with fresh eyes", "fold in this guidance", "review again", "break this down into specs and beads", "make this testable", "check against spec", "what's on the queue", "what's ready", "what's blocking the queue", "create a bead", "file this as work", "run an agent", "dispatch", "use a persona", "how am I doing", "ddx doctor", or mentions any ddx CLI command.
4	---
5	
6	# DDx
7	
8	DDx (Document-Driven Development eXperience) is a CLI platform for
9	document-driven development. It ships a bead tracker (portable work
10	items with acceptance criteria), a task-execution boundary (DDx
11	orchestrates work while Fizeau routes providers/models, catalog lookups,
12	and transcript rendering), a persona system (bindable AI
13	personalities), a library registry (plugins with prompts, templates,
14	personas), and git-aware synchronization. This skill makes any
15	skills-compatible coding agent (Claude Code, OpenAI Codex, Gemini
16	CLI, etc.) understand and operate the DDx surface correctly.
17	
18	## How this skill works
19	
20	The skill body you're reading is an **overview** plus an **intent
21	router**. The real domain guidance lives in `reference/*.md` files.
22	
23	**Directive: before responding to any DDx-related request, read the
24	matching reference file from the router table below. The router is
25	not optional — your answer must be grounded in the reference file's
26	guidance, not this overview alone.**
27	
28	## Vocabulary
29	
30	Single source of truth for DDx terms. Every reference file uses these
31	exact definitions.
32	
33	- **Bead** — a portable work item (task, bug, epic, chore) with
34	  metadata, dependencies, and acceptance criteria. `ddx bead create`.
35	- **Queue** — the set of open beads. The *ready queue* is the subset
36	  with all dependencies closed. `ddx bead ready`, `ddx bead blocked`.
37	- **Ready** — a bead whose dependencies are all closed and which is
38	  eligible to be picked up next. `ddx bead ready`.
39	- **Blocked** — a bead with at least one unclosed dependency.
40	  `ddx bead blocked`.
41	- **Claim** — mark a bead as in-progress by an agent (concurrent-write
42	  protection). `ddx bead update <id> --claim`.
43	- **Close** — mark a bead as done, with evidence (session, commit
44	  SHA). `ddx bead close <id>`. Beads only close on execution outcomes
45	  `success` or `already_satisfied`.
46	- **Run** — one task invocation atom. `ddx run` calls Fizeau `Execute`
47	  once with prompt/config, `MinPower`/`MaxPower` bounds, and optional
48	  passthrough constraints.
49	- **Try** — one bead attempt in an isolated worktree. `ddx try <id>` wraps
50	  `ddx run` with bead prompt resolution, evidence capture, and merge/preserve
51	  finalization.
52	- **Work** — drain the bead queue. `ddx work` picks ready beads and invokes
53	  `ddx try`; it owns queue iteration and retry policy.
54	- **Execution** — a generic DDx execution run (FEAT-010). Includes execution
55	  definitions, execution records, and execution evidence under `.ddx/runs/<id>/`.
56	- **Agent** — an AI coding agent (Claude, Codex, Gemini, etc.)
57	  invoked via a harness. Not a subagent (harness-specific — see
58	  below).
59	- **Harness** — a Fizeau routing concept. DDx may pass `--harness` as
60	  an operator-supplied constraint, but DDx does not validate, rank, fallback, or
61	  branch on harness names.
62	- **Persona** — a Markdown file (YAML frontmatter + body) that
63	  defines an AI personality. DDx injects the body as a system-prompt
64	  addendum to `ddx run`. `ddx persona list/show/bind`.
65	- **Role** — an abstract function (e.g., `code-reviewer`,
66	  `test-engineer`) a workflow can reference. Projects bind roles to
67	  personas.
68	- **Binding** — a project-specific `role: persona` map in
69	  `.ddx/config.yaml` under `persona_bindings`.
70	- **Power bounds** — `MinPower` and optional `MaxPower` integers passed to the
71	  upstream execution service. DDx may raise `MinPower` on eligible retries;
72	  Fizeau owns the concrete harness/provider/model routing.
73	- **Plugin** — a self-contained extension installed to
74	  `.ddx/plugins/<name>/`. The default `ddx` plugin (personas,
75	  prompts, patterns, templates) is auto-installed by `ddx init`.
76	  `ddx install <name>`.
77	- **Skill** — an agentskills.io-standard directory (SKILL.md +
78	  optional `reference/`, `evals/`, `scripts/`). This `ddx` skill is
79	  the one DDx ships. Plugins can ship additional skills.
80	- **Subagent** — a harness-local concept for running a prompt in an
81	  isolated context (Claude Code's `.claude/agents/` + `context:
82	  fork`; Codex's `agents/`; others differ). DDx does not specify subagent
83	  orchestration; that remains harness business.
84	- **Update** — refresh plugin/toolkit content to a newer version.
85	  `ddx update [<plugin>]`.
86	- **Upgrade** — replace the DDx binary with a newer release.
87	  `ddx upgrade`.
88	- **Review** — two distinct concepts. **Bead review**
89	  (`ddx bead review <id>`) grades a completed bead against its
90	  acceptance criteria. **Comparison/adversarial review** is a workflow skill
91	  composition over `ddx run`, not a core quorum flag. See `reference/review.md`.
92	- **Governing artifact** — the document that authorizes a bead's
93	  work: a FEAT-\*, SD-\*, TD-\*, or ADR-\* under `docs/`. Referenced
94	  via `spec-id`.
95	- **Spec-id** — the `spec-id: <ID>` custom field on a bead pointing
96	  at its governing artifact.
97	
98	## Intent router
99	
100	Before responding, read the matching file.
101	
102	| User says / asks about | Read this file |
103	|---|---|
104	| "what should I work on next", "what's blocking the queue", "review with fresh eyes", "fold in this guidance", "review again", "break this down into specs and beads", "make this testable", broad queue orientation or planning | `reference/interactive.md` |
105	| write/plan work, "create a bead", "file this as work", bead metadata, acceptance criteria, dependencies | `reference/beads.md` |
106	| `ddx work`, `ddx try <id>`, "execute bead `<id>`", "drain the queue", "run the next bead", "start the worker", verify-and-close | `reference/work.md` |
107	| "review this", "check against spec", bead review, quorum review, code review, adversarial check | `reference/review.md` |
108	| "assess bead readiness", "score a bead", "triage a failed attempt", "refine a bead", bead authoring lint | `bead-lifecycle/` |
109	| "run an agent", "dispatch", harness/provider/model passthrough, power, effort, "use a persona", role bindings | `reference/agents.md` |
110	| "what's on the queue", "what's ready", "how am I doing", health check, "ddx doctor", sync status | `reference/status.md` |
111	
112	If the intent spans multiple files (e.g., "create a bead and then
113	run it"), read beads.md first, then work.md. If no match, ask the
114	user which concept they mean rather than guessing.
115	
116	## Top-level policy reminders
117	
118	These apply across all DDx operations. Do not restate them in every
119	reference file; do not violate them.
120	
121	- **Never edit `.ddx/beads.jsonl` directly.** All tracker changes go
122	  through `ddx bead create/update/close/dep`. Direct edits corrupt
123	  bead history and cannot be audited.
124	- **Tracker changes are commit-worthy.** After `ddx bead create`,
125	  `update`, `dep add/remove`, or `close`, commit the resulting
126	  `.ddx/beads.jsonl` change — either as a tracker-only commit or
127	  folded into the same commit as related implementation changes.
128	- **Preserve bead-attempt commit history.** Branches containing
129	  `ddx try` / `ddx work` execution commits carry an audit trail. **Never
130	  squash, rebase, filter, or amend** these commits. Use only
131	  `git merge --ff-only` or `git merge --no-ff` when merging.
132	  `gh pr merge --squash` and `--rebase` are forbidden on these
133	  branches.
134	- **Work in worktrees for parallel agents.** Use `wt switch -c
135	  <branch>` (worktrunk) or equivalent to give each concurrent agent
136	  its own isolated checkout. Execute-bead does this automatically;
137	  manual parallel work should too.
138	- **Power-first execution dispatch.** Default to `ddx run`/`ddx try`/`ddx work`
139	  with power bounds. `--harness`, `--provider`, and `--model` are passthrough
140	  constraints only; DDx must not use them for routing policy.
141	
142	## Links out
143	
144	- Full CLI reference: `ddx --help`, `ddx <subcommand> --help`.
145	- Governing feature specs: see `FEAT-*` documents under your
146	  project's `docs/` tree — especially the CLI, beads, agent-service,
147	  executions, and skills features.
148	- Personas README: shipped by the default `ddx` plugin at
149	  `.ddx/plugins/ddx/personas/README.md`.
150	- Open standard this skill conforms to:
151	  [agentskills.io](https://agentskills.io).
